glossary.def: The learner and decision-maker in reinforcement learning — the only part of the system whose behavior is changed by training. It observes states, selects actions according to its policy, and receives rewards; everything outside its direct control is the environment.

The foundational claim that every goal can be cast as the maximization of expected cumulative scalar reward. It is what makes RL a single, general framework, and most practical RL failures — reward hacking, specification gaming — are failures of writing down the reward, not of optimizing it.

The standard formalization of the RL problem: the tuple ⟨S, A, P, R, γ⟩ of states, actions, transition probabilities, rewards, and a discount factor. It is a Markov chain in which transitions depend on a chosen action and every transition emits a reward.

The assumption that the current state is a sufficient statistic of the history — the next state and reward depend only on the present state and action, not on the path taken to reach it. It is a property of how the state is defined, not a law of nature, and engineering a state that satisfies it is much of the art of applying RL.

The quantity an agent maximizes: the sum of future rewards, each weighted by γ raised to its number of steps away, G_t = Σ γ^k R_{t+k+1}. Discounting makes infinite-horizon returns finite, encodes the time value of reward, and sets the agent's effective horizon at roughly 1/(1−γ) steps.

The agent's behavior rule, a (possibly stochastic) mapping π(a | s) from states to actions. It is the object RL ultimately searches for; in a Markov environment an optimal policy that depends only on the current state — not on time or history — is guaranteed to exist.

The expected return from a state (V^π(s)) or a state–action pair (Q^π(s, a)) under a given policy. Values are always relative to a policy; their difference Q^π − V^π is the advantage, and estimating them is how a good policy is found, since acting greedily with respect to Q needs no model of the environment.

The trade-off, unique to learning from interaction, between exploiting the action currently believed best and exploring uncertain actions to improve those beliefs. Because the agent's actions decide what data it sees, an action never tried is never learned from; ε-greedy is the simplest balance, annealing a random-action rate ε toward zero as estimates sharpen.

refs.cite: Sutton, R. S. & Barto, A. G. (2018). Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (2nd ed.). MIT Press.
Bellman, R. (1957). A Markovian Decision Process. Journal of Mathematics and Mechanics 6(5).
Watkins, C. J. C. H. & Dayan, P. (1992). Q-learning. Machine Learning 8.
Mnih, V. et al. (2015). Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning. Nature 518.
Auer, P., Cesa-Bianchi, N. & Fischer, P. (2002). Finite-time Analysis of the Multiarmed Bandit Problem. Machine Learning 47.
Kaelbling, L. P., Littman, M. L. & Moore, A. W. (1996). Reinforcement Learning: A Survey. Journal of Artificial Intelligence Research 4.
